request.question: For which opera was the teacher of Der Bärenhäuter best known?

title: Eli Jaxon-Bear
Hervey Alan
Jan van der Watt
Peter Bos
Engelbert Humperdinck (composer)
Der Bärenhäuter (opera)
Christopher Larkin (conductor)
Rick van der Linden
Nun komm, der Heiden Heiland
Derek Hammond-Stroud
sentences: Eli Jaxon-Bear (born Elliot J. Zeldow in 1947) is an American spiritual teacher and author.
He lives in Ashland, Oregon with his wife, fellow spiritual teacher Gangaji.
Before he met his teacher, Sri H.W.L. Poonja, in 1990, he was best known for his work on the spiritual dimension of the Enneagram.
Following his meeting with Poonjaji he continued to teach, carrying his teacher’s message to the West.
Hervey Alan (22 February 1910 – 12 January 1982) was an English operatic bass and voice teacher.

During his career he sang leading roles with most of Great Britain's major opera institutions, including the Edinburgh Festival, the Glyndebourne Festival, the Royal Opera House, the Sadler's Wells Opera, and the Welsh National Opera.
He is best known for creating the role of Mr. Redburn in the world premiere of Benjamin Britten's "Billy Budd" at the Royal Opera House, London, on 1 December 1951.
Music critic Elizabeth Forbes wrote that his voice was "dark toned, resonant", and "especially effective as Zaccaria in "Nabucco".

Jan Gabriël Van der Watt (born 5 November 1952) is a South African biblical scholar and Bible translator who moved to the Netherlands in 2009 to take up a chair in New Testament and Source texts of early Christianity at Radboud University in Nijmegen.

For a quarter of a century previously, he was professor at the University of Pretoria, where he was named as one of the 100 most influential academic thinkers in the 100-year history of the University of Pretoria, South Africa (see www.up.ac.za under Leading Minds).

Van der Watt was also rated as international acknowledged researcher that is regarded by some of his international peers as international leader in his field, (see www.nrf.ac.za under rated researchers).
Van der Watt is internationally best known for his monograph: Family of the King: Dynamics of Metaphor in the Gosepl According to John.

Petrus Andreas Maria (Peter) Bos (born 20 June 1950 in Amsterdam) is a Dutch film, stage and television actor best known for his role in the 1982 children's television series "De Zevensprong" in which he played the leading part as the teacher Frans van der Steg.
He is also known from the children's film "Knokken voor twee" (directed by Karst van der Meulen) which was released in the same year.
Engelbert Humperdinck (] ; 1 September 1854 – 27 September 1921) was a German composer, best known for his opera "Hansel and Gretel".
Der Bärenhäuter is an 1899 German-language opera by Siegfried Wagner based on the German folk tale Der Bärenhäuter, or the Bearskin man.
As with most of Siegfried Wagner's operas it is more influenced by his teacher Humperdinck than his father Richard Wagner's operas.
Christopher Larkin is an American conductor who is best known for his work within the field of opera.
He was Associate Conductor of the Houston Grand Opera for a number of years where he notably conducted the world premieres of Mark Adamo's "Little Women" and Michael Daugherty’s "Jackie O".
He is also the former Music Director of the New York City Opera National Company.

As a guest conductor, Larkin has worked for numerous opera houses including Wexford Festival Opera, Santa Fe Opera, Washington National Opera, Portland Opera, Florentine Opera, Opera Pacific, Opera Carolina, and Boston Lyric Opera among many others.
He has also worked as an Assistant Conductor and vocal coach for the Metropolitan Opera, San Francisco Opera, Opera Company of Philadelphia, Vancouver Opera, and the Canadian Opera Company.
Rick van der Linden (5 August 1946, Badhoevedorp, North Holland - 22 January 2006, Groningen) was a Dutch composer and keyboardist.
Van der Linden first gained fame as a member of Ekseption, but he played in several other bands including most notably Trace, as well as solo.
Van der Linden was best known for his reworkings of classical music in a pop music domain, often with jazz improvisations.

"Nun komm, der Heiden Heiland " (original: "Nu kom der Heyden heyland ", English: "Now come, Saviour of the heathens") is a Lutheran chorale of 1524 with words written by Martin Luther, based on "Veni redemptor gentium " by Ambrose.
It was printed in the Erfurt "Enchiridion" of 1524.
The chorale was used as the prominent hymn for the first Sunday of Advent for centuries.

It was used widely in organ settings by Protestant baroque composers, most notably Johann Sebastian Bach: he set it as the opening chorale prelude BWV 599 of Orgelbüchlein; and three times—as BWV 659 (one of his best known organ compositions), BWV 660 and BWV 661—in his Great Eighteen Chorale Preludes.

Bach used the hymn in his chorale cantata "Nun komm, der Heiden Heiland", BWV 62 (1724) and in the opening chorale fantasia of his earlier cantata "Nun komm, der Heiden Heiland", BWV 61 , BWV 61 (1714).
Max Reger composed a chorale prelude as No. 29 of his 52 Chorale Preludes, Op. 67 in 1902.

Derek Hammond-Stroud, OBE (10 January 192614 May 2012) was an English baritone opera singer best known for his performances of German lieder and his international performances in opera, particularly the roles of Alberich in Wagner's "Der Ring des Nibelungen", Herr Faninal in "Der Rosenkavalier" and Beckmesser in "Die Meistersinger von Nürnberg".
He also made recordings, including a series of recordings of the Gilbert and Sullivan patter roles.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: For which opera was the teacher of Der Bärenhäuter best known?
hop1_docs: Der Bärenhäuter (opera) | Der Bärenhäuter is an 1899 German-language opera by Siegfried Wagner based on the German folk tale Der Bärenhäuter, or the Bearskin man.  As with most of Siegfried Wagner's operas it is more influenced by his teacher Humperdinck than his father Richard Wagner's operas.
Der Bärenhäuter | Der Bärenhäuter (German "Bearskin man") may refer to:
Bearskin (film) | Der Bärenhäuter is a German movie from the year 1986, based on the fairy tale "Bearskin".

Peter Bos | Petrus Andreas Maria (Peter) Bos (born 20 June 1950 in Amsterdam) is a Dutch film, stage and television actor best known for his role in the 1982 children's television series "De Zevensprong" in which he played the leading part as the teacher Frans van der Steg.  He is also known from the children's film "Knokken voor twee" (directed by Karst van der Meulen) which was released in the same year.

Der Prozeß (opera) | Der Prozeß ("English: The Trial ") is a German-language opera in two parts, divided into nine scenes, with music by Gottfried von Einem and a libretto by Boris Blacher and , based on the novel by Franz Kafka.  Composed over the period 1950 to 1952, this was von Einem's second opera.  He dedicated it to the psychologist and theologian Oskar Pfister, who had been his therapist, and to his former teacher, Karl Christian Jensen.

Derek Hammond-Stroud | Derek Hammond-Stroud, OBE (10 January 192614 May 2012) was an English baritone opera singer best known for his performances of German lieder and his international performances in opera, particularly the roles of Alberich in Wagner's "Der Ring des Nibelungen", Herr Faninal in "Der Rosenkavalier" and Beckmesser in "Die Meistersinger von Nürnberg".  He also made recordings, including a series of recordings of the Gilbert and Sullivan patter roles.

Pomona (opera) | Der Sieg der fruchtbaren Pomona (The Victory of Fruitful Pomona) is a German-language opera by Reinhard Keiser to a libretto by Christian Heinrich Postel which premiered at the Hamburg Opera on 19 October 1702, for the birthday of Frederick IV of Denmark.  The plot was based on the legend of Pomona and Jupiter, and the libretto was the last of a series which the elderly poet Postel had provided to the young Keiser during the five previous opera seasons.  The opera was the first of four Keiser operas dedicated to the Danish crown.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - "Der Bärenhäuter is an 1899 German-language opera by Siegfried Wagner" (passage [1]).
  - "more influenced by his teacher Humperdinck" (passage [1]).
  - Passage [1] also mentions "his father Richard Wagner" (passage [1]) but does not link to Humperdinck's full name.
- Ambiguities:
  - The passages only use the single token "Humperdinck" (no given name). Candidate page variants are not present in the passages (e.g., "Engelbert Humperdinck (composer)" is not shown). Do not assume which "Humperdinck" is intended.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (as given in the passages): "Humperdinck".
  - Missing fact needed to answer the question: the specific opera title (and canonical attribution/year if helpful) for which "Humperdinck" is described as best known.
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open "Engelbert Humperdinck" page (check lede/infobox and "Works"/"Notable works" or lede wording for "best known for"); search keywords: Engelbert Humperdinck composer "best known for" "opera".
  2) If the name is ambiguous, open "Engelbert Humperdinck (disambiguation)" to confirm the composer vs. other persons.
  3) Open the opera page likely associated (e.g., "Hänsel und Gretel (opera)") and check the lede/infobox for composer attribution and notability statements.
  4) Optionally open "Siegfried Wagner" page lede/Career to confirm the teacher reference and any named first name of Humperdinck.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
response.hop2_query: Engelbert Humperdinck composer best known for opera
hop2_docs: Engelbert Humperdinck (composer) | Engelbert Humperdinck (] ; 1 September 1854 – 27 September 1921) was a German composer, best known for his opera "Hansel and Gretel".

Hansel and Gretel (opera) | Hansel and Gretel (German: Hänsel und Gretel ) is an opera by nineteenth-century composer Engelbert Humperdinck, who described it as a "Märchenoper " (fairy-tale opera).  The libretto was written by Humperdinck's sister, Adelheid Wette, based on the Grimm brothers' fairy tale "Hansel and Gretel".  It is much admired for its folk music-inspired themes, one of the most famous being the "Abendsegen " ("Evening Benediction") from act 2.

Matthias Hues | Matthias Hues (born 14 February 1959) is a German-born, naturalized American actor and martial artist.  He was born in Waltrop, Germany, to Dr. Josef Hues and Maria Humperdinck Hues who still resides in Germany.  Maria is the niece of Engelbert Humperdinck, composer of the opera "Hansel and Gretel".

Brother, Come and Dance with Me | "Brother, Come And Dance With Me" is a popular children’s song that was originally written by the German composer Engelbert Humperdinck for his famous opera, "Hänsel und Gretel" (ca. 1891).

Manfred Gurlitt | Manfred Gurlitt (6 September 1890 – 29 April 1972) was a German opera composer and conductor.  He studied composition with Engelbert Humperdinck and conducting with Karl Muck.  He spent most of his career in Japan.
Engelbert Humperdinck discography | This is a discography of the British singer Engelbert Humperdinck who made his professional debut in 1967.
Hansel and Gretel (1963 film) | Hansel and Gretel is a 1963 Australian TV adaptation of the opera "Hansel and Gretel" by Engelbert Humperdinck.
hop2_titles: Brother, Come and Dance with Me
response.summary_2: Der Bärenhäuter's teacher was Engelbert Humperdinck. Engelbert Humperdinck was a German composer best known for his opera "Hansel and Gretel".
